label: Class · Implicit interactions
subtitle: Wendy Ju framework, affordances, smart classroom design
verdict: Strong week 2 lecture. Theoretical framework established, students primed for project work. Pacing uneven — first half rich, second half rushed.

sections.label: Warm-up · current events
sections.summary: AI agents discussion (the “clodbots” article); developer turning their own agent off because it took agency away — perfect framing for the class theme.

sections.label: History of smart objects
sections.summary: Mechanical Turk, the 1960s home computer, Xerox PARC. Sets the lineage.

sections.label: Core theory · Wendy Ju framework
sections.summary: Implicit/explicit interaction quadrant chart. Axis labels swapped on the slide — caused visible confusion mid-lecture.

sections.label: Group activity · quadrant mapping
sections.summary: Three groups mapped interactions onto the chart. Worked beautifully — substantive ideas across all groups.

sections.label: Group presentations
sections.summary: Each team shared. Genuine discoveries surfaced — the “terminal as the new smart object” insight came from a student.

sections.label: Affordances · Gibson, Norman
sections.summary: Compressed. Should arguably be its own session.

sections.label: AI as material vs agent
sections.summary: Real paradigm-shift discussion. ~10 min on AI affordances pulled away from the framework.

sections.label: Wrap-up & homework
sections.summary: Homework explanation; canvas access flagged at the very end.

assignments.title: Interaction sketches
assignments.spec: 3–5 sketches of smart-classroom interactions; at least one strong, all three should explore implicit/explicit positioning.
rubric: Maps onto the Wendy Ju quadrant
Names a beneficiary and a possible harm
Identifies signifiers, feedback, and failure mode
assignments.ambiguity: Original brief said “3–5”, then “at least one good”, then “at least three”. Clarify on Slack.

assignments.title: Conversation design PDF
assignments.spec: Read the conversation-design PDF; come prepared to discuss who benefits and who is harmed.
rubric: Quote at least one passage from the PDF

assignments.title: Who benefits / who is harmed?
assignments.spec: Pick one of your sketches and write the harm side honestly — not a marketing pitch.
rubric: Names a specific population at risk
Names the assumption the design hides

grammar.issue: Slide axis labels swapped (person aware ↔ unaware)
grammar.fix: Update the master deck; re-render before week 3.

grammar.issue: Homework brief ambiguous — count of sketches drifted (3–5, then 1, then 3)
grammar.fix: Pin the count to “3 sketches, one developed”. Clarify on Slack.

grammar.issue: Canvas course wasn't published — student raised at end of class
grammar.fix: Publish Canvas before next class; verify at start of week-3 session.

grammar.issue: AI affordances tangent (~10 min) drifted from the implicit/explicit framework
grammar.fix: Move AI material to its own scheduled session (“The Elephant in the Room”).

grammar.issue: “The Elephant in the Room” AI talk teased multiple times, never given

ideas.title: Culture Translator
ideas.summary: Detect American cultural references, surface context for international students.

ideas.title: Who Presents First Spinner
ideas.summary: When nobody volunteers, the room reads silence and picks someone gently.

ideas.title: Energy/Fatigue Detection
ideas.summary: Order coffee or snacks when students look tired — implicit care.

ideas.title: Communal Note-Taking
ideas.summary: Shared, projected workspace — replace siloed laptops with one canvas.

ideas.title: CR Cleanup Reminder
ideas.summary: Alert the class rep before they leave without cleaning up.

ideas.title: Smart glasses / Adaptive lighting
ideas.summary: Glasses, language translation, light adapting to mood.

engagement.label: Personal connection
engagement.note: International student tied culture-translator idea to lived experience.

engagement.label: Ethical thinking
engagement.note: Class flagged the “embarrassing the sleeper” problem unprompted.

actions.title: Fix the X/Y axis error in the quadrant slide
Schedule “Elephant in the Room” AI talk
Follow up on Culture Translator + Communal Notes

quotes.speaker: Mark Weiser
quotes.text: The most profound technologies are those that disappear.

quotes.speaker: Brett (paraphrase)
quotes.text: The best interaction is no interaction.

quotes.speaker: Tobias Revell
quotes.text: Technologies don't preexist or emerge autonomously. They are products of social dynamics, atmospheres, aspirations.

quotes.speaker: Brett
quotes.text: We're not talking about building apps. We're talking about building connections and experiences between people.
